edges.source: Genetic Heterogeneity and Cortical Development Disruption
Genetic Heterogeneity and Cortical Development Disruption

nodes.description: Complete or partial absence of the corpus callosum, one of the three classic features of the Aicardi triad. Complete agenesis in ~72% of cases.

evidence.snippet: Aicardi syndrome (AS) is characterized by a triad of callosal agenesis, infantile spasms and chorioretinal 'lacunae'.

nodes.description: Underdevelopment of the inferior cerebellar vermis, contributing to posterior fossa abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: underdevelopment of the inferior cerebellar vermis tonsils were also observed.

nodes.description: Pathognomonic chorioretinal lacunae visible on fundoscopy, the third component of the classic Aicardi triad. These are essentially specific to Aicardi syndrome among congenital disorders.

evidence.snippet: typical choroidal lacunae are always present and are pathognomonic.

nodes.description: Pathognomonic chorioretinal lacunae are depigmented, well-circumscribed lesions of the retinal pigment epithelium and choroid. These lesions result from thinning of the choroid and sclera with degeneration of overlying photoreceptor cells. They are bilateral, clustered around the optic nerve head and posterior pole, and remain stable over time.

evidence.snippet: Aicardi syndrome is characterized by agenesis of the corpus callosum, chorioretinal lacunae, and infantile spasms. The authors describe the case of a girl with chorioretinal lacunae, seizures, and cerebral cyst, but a normal corpus callosum. Incomplete forms of Aicardi syndrome are reported in the literature, but typical choroidal lacunae are always present and are pathognomonic.

nodes.description: Complete or partial absence of the corpus callosum, the major white matter tract connecting the two cerebral hemispheres. Complete agenesis is found in approximately 72% of affected individuals. This disrupts interhemispheric communication and manifests as a characteristic split-brain pattern on EEG, contributing to seizure intractability and neurodevelopmental impairment.

evidence.snippet: Aicardi syndrome (AS) is characterized by a triad of callosal agenesis, infantile spasms and chorioretinal 'lacunae'.

nodes.description: Widespread neuronal migration defects including polymicrogyria (predominantly frontal and perisylvian), periventricular and subcortical heterotopia, and intracranial cysts. These likely result from partial cortical necrosis during development rather than a primary migratory disturbance.

evidence.snippet: The neuropathological examination of the brain of a 4-month-old girl with Aicardi syndrome (infantile spasms, chorioretinal lacunae, psychomotor retardation, agenesis of the corpus callosum and vertebral anomalies) showed agenesis of the corpus callosum, agenesis of the anterior commissure and abnormal orientation of the hippocampal formation, in addition to periventricular cerebral nodules and extensive non-laminated cortical polymicrogyria of the fronto-parietal regions and gyrus cinguli of both hemispheres.
The brain malformation is complex with cortical migration abnormalities, often cystic formations and sometimes choroid plexus papillomas

nodes.description: Difficulty swallowing, frequently necessitating tube feeding support.
Characteristic split-brain EEG pattern with dissociated suppression-burst activity, reflecting independent electrical activity between cerebral hemispheres due to absent corpus callosum.

Although the causal gene remains unidentified, recent genomic analyses have identified rare de novo variants in KMT2B, SLF1, SMARCB1, SZT2, and WNT8B in a small subset of clinically diagnosed individuals. These variants are candidate findings rather than established recurrent causes, but they converge on molecular pathways central to cortical development. Earlier candidate genes TEAD1 and OCEL1 were not confirmed in an independent cohort of 38 patients. Non-random X-inactivation in 33% of patients supports the longstanding X-linked hypothesis, while the absence of a recurrent X-linked gene in sequencing studies leaves autosomal or mosaic mechanisms with sex-limited expression unresolved.
nodes.id: Genetic Heterogeneity and Cortical Development Disruption

evidence.snippet: Our findings suggest that AIC is genetically heterogeneous with implicated genes converging on molecular pathways central to cortical development.
We did not detect the previously reported or any other deleterious variants in any of the analyzed samples.

nodes.description: Significant delays in all areas of development. Only approximately 17% of affected girls acquire the ability to speak words.
Decreased muscle tone, commonly present in infancy.
Early-onset infantile spasms are a defining feature, typically appearing at median age 2.2 months. Spasms frequently occur in clusters and tend to increase in frequency over time.

evidence.snippet: Median age at disease onset was 2.2 months. Median diagnostic delay was 1 month. Mortality was estimated with 6% at 1 and 17% at 5 years of age.

nodes.description: Severe to profound intellectual disability is present in virtually all cases, though emerging evidence suggests a broader phenotypic spectrum with a small proportion showing milder cognitive involvement.

evidence.snippet: The outcome of AS is severe, with a high early mortality, considerable morbidity and a generally poor developmental outcome. However, the spectrum of AS seems broader than previously defined with a small proportion of the affected girls only moderately or mildly retarded.

nodes.description: Intracranial cysts, typically interhemispheric or periventricular, are a common finding and included in revised diagnostic criteria as a major feature.

evidence.snippet: The brain malformation is complex with cortical migration abnormalities, often cystic formations and sometimes choroid plexus papillomas

nodes.description: Abnormally small head circumference, associated with worse developmental outcomes.
Abnormally small eye size, present in approximately 20% of affected individuals.
Gaps or defects in the optic disc and nerve, occurring in approximately 44% of patients.

evidence.snippet: the eye anomalies, often feature a coloboma in addition to the lacunae

nodes.description: Collections of gray matter in abnormal locations within white matter or ventricular regions, reflecting incomplete neuronal migration.

evidence.snippet: periventricular cerebral nodules and extensive non-laminated cortical polymicrogyria

nodes.description: Excessive folding of the cerebral cortex creating abnormally small gyri, with predominantly frontal and perisylvian distribution. Present in nearly all affected individuals.

evidence.snippet: extensive non-laminated cortical polymicrogyria of the fronto-parietal regions and gyrus cinguli of both hemispheres

nodes.description: Abnormal curvature of the spine, developing in approximately one-third of affected children. Scoliosis in Aicardi syndrome is rapidly progressive and bracing is ineffective, often necessitating early surgical intervention.

evidence.snippet: Scoliosis in ACS can represent a clinically significant problem that is underdiagnosed and overshadowed by the other severe medical complications associated with the syndrome. Our data suggest that scoliosis in patients with ACS is rapidly progressive and bracing is ineffective

nodes.description: Epilepsy in Aicardi syndrome is usually refractory to treatment and often evolves into focal, tonic, and myoclonic seizure types. Callosal agenesis and other structural brain abnormalities likely impair normal inhibitory signaling between hemispheres and promote seizure propagation.

evidence.snippet: In addition to the classic findings, patients typically experience intractable epilepsy of multiple seizure types, profound mental retardation, and costovertebral anomalies.

nodes.description: Beyond infantile spasms, patients develop multiple seizure types including focal, tonic, myoclonic, atonic, and absence seizures. Nearly universally refractory to antiseizure medications.

evidence.snippet: patients typically experience intractable epilepsy of multiple seizure types, profound mental retardation, and costovertebral anomalies.

nodes.description: Enlargement of the cerebral ventricles, reflecting underlying brain structural abnormalities.
